narrative:
Passing light showers moved through late Thursday night. We are left with a pleasant Friday, a couple of degrees less warm than Thursday. Saturday, we'll be back to near-record warmth. That is the garnish on 9 straight days of unseasonably warm weather for mid-April.

A potent but moisture-starved front blows through after midnight Saturday night. Expect widely scattered showers, mostly after midnight Saturday night and ending quickly Sunday morning . Chilly air blows in behind the front. Temperatures Sunday and Monday are 25 degrees cooler than late this week--that will be a shock. Freezing conditions are a decent bet in valleys Monday and Tuesday mornings.

Two Warm Days; Then Ouch

Last Updated Fri April 17 at 10:51AM by Ray Russell

Passing light showers moved through late Thursday night. We are left with a pleasant Friday, a couple of degrees less warm than Thursday. Saturday, we'll be back to near-record warmth. That is the garnish on 9 straight days of unseasonably warm weather for mid-April.

A potent but moisture-starved front blows through after midnight Saturday night. Expect widely scattered showers, mostly after midnight Saturday night and ending quickly Sunday morning . Chilly air blows in behind the front. Temperatures Sunday and Monday are 25 degrees cooler than late this week--that will be a shock. Freezing conditions are a decent bet in valleys Monday and Tuesday mornings.

The RaysWeather.Com Photo Contest is underway for photos to appear in the 2027 Blue Ridge Calendar. We are open for submissions through April 19. We broke all records with 2026 calendar sales and look to top that with the 2027 calendar — it's our 24th calendar. Come on! Hit us with your best shot!

Forecast Discussion

Friday with temperatures a couple of degrees less warm than yesterday.

Late in the day, as the trough exits into the Atlantic, high pressure reasserts itself from the south. That continues into Saturday, putting us back to near-record warmth for one last day.

A game-changing front blows through from the west after midnight Saturday night. Goodbye summer temperatures! A few showers (possibly thunder) accompany the front, mostly after midnight Saturday night and ending early Sunday morning.

Sunday is windy and MUCH cooler. Chilly air settles in Sunday night and Monday. We’ll be 25 degrees cooler Sunday and Monday compared to our recent warmth. Freezing conditions are possible Sunday night into early Monday morning and again in valleys late Monday night into early Tuesday.

The trough in the eastern U.S. lifts northeast beginning Tuesday, gradually putting us into a warmer flow. Dry weather continues, probably through the workweek, with progressively warmer temperatures (but not as warm as this week).

Drought quietly continues to be the big weather story. Maybe we can get some showers into the area by next Sunday.

Apr 16, 2026
An Update On Spring's Colors

Folks, we made it through another winter. This isn’t to say that snowflakes can’t still fly (in fact, April almost always brings at least a light high-elevation event). But, we’ve logged several days in the 70s across the mountains and 80s in the Foothills over the past month or so, and there are leaves and blooms galore in the southern Foothills. More noticeable spring color is limited to areas below 3000 feet or so at the time of this writing (April 10th), but notable progress is being made to our south. Our own Jack Kendrick took the cover image around 1300 feet in elevation in far northern South Carolina on April 4th, just a couple miles from the state line.

It is a surefire sign that spring is on its way. Lime greens and dusty yellows are a welcome sight for spring and summer lovers further south. Meanwhile, buds are more plentiful in the mountains, but trees remain bare for many of us. After such an incredibly warm run this March, you may be wondering how that affects the status of spring and its progress up the mountain. Well, thankfully, there’s an index for that! The National Phenology Network utilizes on-the-ground observations of plant species that are usually the first to show their leaves (namely honeysuckle and lilac) to track the progression of spring. You may be thinking as you glance out the window, depending on your location, “Those trees still look awfully bare to me.” They certainly are, but the First Leaf Index does not use our trees to track spring. Keep that in mind when viewing this map:

This map shows how early or late spring is at a given location. For much of the Carolinas, there is actually a bit of a lag this year compared to others, perhaps due to the rather dry winter we have had, but the mountains are bright red – indicating an early leaf-out of the first spring indicator species. This certainly owes to the warm middle of February and warm March, despite the drought conditions that persist over our area. So, while you may not be seeing the results you think of when someone says “spring has arrived,” rest assured that it is moving quickly, and we are less than a month away from areas below 4000 feet seeing widespread spring fever. Here’s a rough guideline of what to expect from the next several weeks:

April 12-18 | Lower valleys of Southwestern NC begin to leaf out, with noticeable green confined to 3000’ or lower. Foothills spring is in full swing, with blooms and trees greening up.

April 19-25 | Buds can be found up to 4000-5000’. Many trees in the Foothills are almost completely leafed out, and High Country valleys are showing more spring.

April 26-May 2 | First greens progress up to roughly 4000’. Low elevation wildflowers are starting to really get going, but the highest elevations are just getting some of their first buds.

May 3-9 | All elevations have signs of spring, with lower valleys and Foothills trees just about onto summer status. Mountain laurel is plentiful below 4000’.

May 10-16 | Pink Catawba rhododendron blooms are widespread below 3000’, with some higher than that. Above 5000’, spring colors are peaking, with transition to fully green not far behind.

May 17-23 | Blooms can be found at most elevations, but are still limited at highest spots. High Country valleys are leafed out.

May 24-30 | The last of the deciduous trees above 5000’ are leafed out, with only blooms left to progress upward. Summer is underway.

The animation below shows (roughly) weekly images at 12:00PM from mid-April to June 1st, 2025 courtesy of the National Park Service webcam on Purchase Knob (5086’), just inside the Great Smoky Mountains in Haywood County. To get the best feel for the progress of spring, watch the trees on the hillside in the foreground.
